era_context: The year 1491 falls in the final stages of the Spanish Reconquista, with the siege of Granada ongoing. In Europe, the Italian Renaissance is flourishing; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating Mediterranean dominance; and the nation-states of Spain, Portugal, France, and England are consolidating power. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules a unified China; the Joseon Dynasty governs Korea; and the Mughal Empire is beginning its consolidation in Central Asia. In the Americas, the Inca Empire is at its height under Tupac Inca Yupanqui. The year represents the threshold between the medieval and early modern worlds, with the Reconquista's completion imminent.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (sparse written records; primarily oral traditions and later Spanish accounts)

events.title: Siege of Granada (final phase of the Reconquista)
events.description: In 1491, the Spanish monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella intensified their siege of Granada, the last Muslim kingdom in Iberia. The siege, which began in 1482, reached its climax in 1491–1492. Spanish forces surrounded the city, cutting off supplies and reinforcements. The Emir of Granada, Muhammad XII (Boabdil), faced mounting pressure from both the Spanish besiegers and internal factional conflicts within the emirate. Negotiations for surrender began in late 1491, and the city would fall on January 2, 1492. The siege of Granada represented the culmination of nearly 800 years of Christian reconquest of the Iberian Peninsula. The siege was extensively documented in Spanish chronicles and represented a major military and symbolic victory for Christian Spain.

sources.name: Spanish royal chronicles (Crónica de los Reyes Católicos)

events.certainty_note: The siege of Granada in 1491–1492 is precisely documented in Spanish chronicles and diplomatic records. The intensification of the siege in 1491 is well-attested.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast
events.description: In 1491, Portuguese explorers and merchants continued their systematic exploration of the West African coast, seeking the sea route to India. The Portuguese had established trading posts and forts along the coast, including São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle, established 1482). In 1491, Portuguese expeditions were probing further south, seeking to round the Cape of Good Hope and reach the Indian Ocean. The Portuguese exploration was driven by commercial ambitions (spice trade), religious zeal (conversion of non-Christians), and the desire to outflank Muslim middlemen in the Indian Ocean trade. The Portuguese exploration would ultimately lead to the establishment of a global maritime empire and the beginning of European dominance in global trade.
key_figures: Portuguese merchants and explorers (names often unrecorded)
events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast in the 1490s is well-documented in Portuguese archives. Specific events in 1491 are less precisely dated than the broader trend of coastal expansion.

events.title: Ming Dynasty administrative consolidation
events.description: In 1491, the Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors (the Yongle Emperor died in 1424) continued to consolidate administrative control over China. The year falls within a period of relative stability and prosperity. The Ming court was functioning smoothly, the civil service examination system was established, and China was experiencing economic growth. The treasure voyages of Zheng He were in their final stages. In 1491, the Ming Dynasty was at the height of its power and influence, though the court was increasingly focused on internal administration rather than maritime exploration.
events.certainty_note: No specific transformative event is documented for China in 1491. This entry reflects the broader era context of Ming stability and administrative consolidation.

events.title: Tupac Inca Yupanqui's reign in the Inca Empire
events.description: In 1491, Tupac Inca Yupanqui (r. 1471–1493) continued his reign as the ruler of the Inca Empire. Tupac was one of the greatest Inca rulers, having expanded the empire to its greatest territorial extent and consolidated administrative control. In 1491, the empire was at the height of its power and territorial extent. Tupac's reign saw the consolidation of Inca administrative systems, the construction of roads and infrastructure, and the integration of conquered territories. The empire remained stable and prosperous, unaware of the impending arrival of European diseases and conquistadors that would devastate the Inca population in the following decades.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers, oral tradition recorded by Spanish chroniclers)
Cieza de León, Pedro de. Chronicle of Peru (16th century, based on oral accounts)

events.certainty_note: The Inca did not use written records; dating relies on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades after the events. No specific event is documented for 1491; this entry reflects the broader era context of Tupac's reign.

events.title: Ottoman consolidation in the Mediterranean
events.description: In 1491, the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II (r. 1481–1512) continued its consolidation of power in the Mediterranean and the Balkans. The Ottomans had recently conquered Bosnia (1463) and were consolidating control over the Balkans. In 1491, Ottoman forces were engaged in campaigns against Venice and other Christian powers for control of Mediterranean trade routes and strategic ports. The Ottoman navy was becoming increasingly dominant in the Mediterranean, challenging Venetian and Genoese merchant fleets. This period saw the consolidation of Ottoman power as a major Mediterranean and European force, setting the stage for the empire's expansion into the 16th century.

sources.name: Venetian diplomatic correspondence (State Archives of Venice)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the 1490s is well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources. Specific campaigns in 1491 are less precisely dated than broader trends.

events.title: Joseon Dynasty cultural development (Korea)
events.description: In 1491, the Joseon Dynasty of Korea was in a period of cultural and intellectual development under King Seongjong (r. 1469–1494). The Joseon court was promoting Confucian scholarship, the development of Korean language and script (Hangul, though not yet widely adopted), and the compilation of historical records. The period saw advances in printing, astronomy, and medicine. In 1491, the Joseon court was stable and prosperous, with the king nearing the end of his reign. The transition of power would occur in 1494, marking the beginning of a more turbulent period under Yeongsan-gun.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Joseon court records are meticulous and well-preserved. No specific transformative event is documented for 1491; this entry reflects the broader era context of Seongjong's reign.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The siege of Granada in 1491 is well-documented and uncontroversial. The broader context of Portuguese exploration, Ming consolidation, Ottoman expansion, and Joseon cultural development are all supported by historiographic consensus.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1491 is moderate for Europe (diplomatic archives, chronicles, printed materials) but sparse for Asia, Africa, and the Americas. European sources dominate because of the survival of written records and the bias of modern historiography toward European perspectives. The siege of Granada is extensively documented in Spanish chronicles and diplomatic records. The Ottoman expansion is known through Ottoman court records and Venetian diplomatic correspondence. However, the Inca Empire's history relies entirely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades after the events, introducing significant dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa is almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources. The Portuguese exploration of the African coast is documented in Portuguese archives but with limited detail about specific events in 1491. Modern scholarship has increasingly emphasized non-European perspectives, but the imbalance in surviving sources remains.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Granada in 1491 led to the city's fall on January 2, 1492
Tupac's reign in 1491 led to his death and Huayna Capac's succession in 1492–1493
Seongjong's reign in 1491 led to his death and Yeongsan-gun's accession in 1494
